So, do we have a deal — or not?

China’s oil stockpiling strategy vindicated by Hormuz crisis

China has cut crude oil imports and refinery processing volumes to multi-year lows in response to the war between the United States and Iran and the interruption of oil imports via the Strait of Hormuz.

Petroleum planners appear to have suspended crude imports into strategic storage and have likely run down commercial stocks of both crude and refined products while restricting fuel exports.

The enormous inventories of crude and refined fuels China accumulated before the war have provided significant flexibility and allowed it to step back from global markets temporarily ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

U.S. GASOLINE stocks have fallen to the lowest for the time of year since 2014. Stocks have depleted by 38 million barrels since the start of the war with Iran, three times faster than the average over the previous ten years. As a result, inventories were 20 million barrels (-9% or -1.82 standard deviations) below the ten-year average on June 5 down from a surplus of 5 million (+2% or +0.99σ) on February 27:

Wait, so Fujairah and ARA fuel oil storage is at tank bottom. Singapore is 8 million bbls or 3 weeks from tank bottom?

The latest long liquidation across hard assets has been broad-based, highlighting a macro-driven adjustment rather than weakness tied to any single commodity. Rising inflation expectations, higher bond yields, a stronger dollar, and growing speculation that the Federal Reserve may need to maintain a restrictive stance for longer have combined to pressure the sector.

The Bloomberg Commodity precious metals index has fallen 11.4% so far this month and is now down 5% year-to-date. Industrial metals have proven somewhat more resilient, declining 3.8% on the month while still holding gains of around 11% for the year.

While the less liquid silver and platinum markets have led the sell-off, with month-to-date declines of 15.5% and 13.5% respectively, losses have been widespread across the complex. Even copper, which remains one of the strongest-performing commodities over the past year, has come under pressure. COMEX HG copper is however only down just 2.3% this month and continues to hold above key support around USD 6.15 per pound, while maintaining a premium over LME copper as traders await the end-June deadline for the US administration's review of potential copper tariffs.
https://t.co/4JLJ4NY9ac

Gold’s weeklong slump extended in Asia, with prices tumbling towards USD 4,000 - a seven-month low - before rebounding into the European session. Selling accelerated last week after gold broke below its 200-day moving average, currently at USD 4,442, triggering technical momentum selling and forcing some long liquidation. The decline gathered further pace on Wednesday following the strong US inflation print and renewed gains in oil prices after fresh attacks in the Middle East. Bullion has now corrected 38.2% of the 2022–2026 rally, with a sustained break below USD 4,000 potentially opening the way towards USD 3,600, the 50% retracement. Meanwhile a move above USD 4,250 would be the first sign of stabilisation.

Gold’s USD 1,570 tumble from the January peak, and not least its weakness during the Middle East conflict, has by now triggered a near-complete reset in investor sentiment. This is reflected in falling futures open interest, ETF outflows, and a slowdown in physical demand. While the longer-term supportive themes are likely to reassert themselves once the inflation outlook stabilises, the near-term outlook remains clouded by speculative selling, long liquidation, and rate hike fears.

Consumer prices rose 0.5% in May, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Wednesday, lifting annual inflation to 4.2% from 3.8%—the first 4%-handle increase in three years.

But the heat was mostly contained in energy: It accounted for more than 60% of the monthly damage, with gasoline jumping 7% from April and 40.5% over the year as the war in Iran chokes the Strait of Hormuz and drives oil prices higher.

If you strip out food and energy from the “core” CPI, it rose just 0.2% in May and 2.9% over the year, softer than expected and suggesting that the spillover was relatively limited. That’s the number the Fed actually watches, and it isn’t flashing the overheating signs that could force its hand.

Overheating was the fear over this print, not just from the Iran war but from AI. Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee has warned that the AI boom could heat the economy before it delivers any productivity gains, as the costs of the build-out grow. China’s economy showed a version of this overnight, with wholesale inflation near a four-year high on AI-driven demand. But May’s consumer data doesn’t show this yet; the hot part is still the oil shock.

Yet an inflation rate above 4% makes a rate cut harder for new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh to justify, especially after a surprisingly strong May jobs report. The risk now is that the oil shock, like the previous supply shocks that have battered the economy since the pandemic, proves to be sticky.

It’s an awkward print for Warsh in particular. He was confirmed in May in the closest Fed chair vote in modern history, and critics accuse him of being installed largely because Trump expects him to cut rates. Warsh, for his part, has argued that productivity gains from AI will allow the economy to grow faster without spurring inflation, thereby enabling the Fed to lower borrowing costs. 

But the American public is showing signs of fatigue with five years of above-2% inflation. Consumer sentiment fell to a record low in May, marking its third straight monthly drop, with 57% of Americans reporting that high prices are eroding their finances. Wednesday’s print showed that wages rose just 3.4% over the year, meaning that 4.2% inflation is beginning to outpace workers’ pay gains as prices rise in gas, electricity, food, and medical care. 

The market has gotten the message. After May’s strong jobs report and Goldman Sachs pulling its 2026 rate-cut forecast, traders began pricing out a rate cut and pricing in a hike. As of Wednesday, the CME FedWatch tool priced a 63% chance of a quarter-point increase by October, a near-total reversal from the cut consensus that dominated Wall Street weeks ago.

Jeffrey Roach, chief economist at LPL Financial, expects the central bank to “remain on hold while removing any bias toward additional easing,” basically stripping out the signal that cuts are coming.

In the early days of the Iran war, analysts held the grim prediction that crude oil prices would top $200 a barrel, nearly  triple pre-war prices. But more than three months into the conflict, their fears have not materialized, and analysts have China’s trade activity to thank for it. 

“As the conflict enters its fourth month, one development stands out: prices have become remarkably calm,” JPMorgan analysts wrote in a note this week.

Oil prices hovered around $94 a barrel on Wednesday following President Donald Trump’s announcement thatIran will “pay the price” for its laggard progress in brokering a peace deal. Wednesday’s oil price is still below the $104 per barrel from a month ago. 

China’s plummeting imports have effectively shielded oil prices from increasing, analysts said. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which about 20% of the world’s oil supply is traded, has created the largest energy disruption in global history. But China’s reliance on its strategic oil reserves, with its total stockpiles touching 1.4 billion barrels, has helped steady what could be a crisis of even greater magnitude. 

The country has gone from importing around 11 million barrels a day on average for the last five years to about 7.8 million barrels a day in May, according to customs data—its lowest levels in nearly a decade. China’s import reduction makes up about 74% of the world’s decrease in global crude oil trade, according to the JPMorgan note.

Societe Generale analysts led by Mike Haigh, head of FIC and commodity research at the bank, noted China as the market’s “key rebalancing force.” In a note published on Monday, analysts noted today’s 14% loss in global crude supply as a result of the Strait of Hormuz closure has increased prices about 30%. By comparison, the 1973 OPEC oil embargo disrupted 7% of the global crude supply, yet prices skyrocketed by more than 130%. 

How long can China cushion energy costs?

China’s ability to help keep oil prices lower may be limited, however. 

Michal Meidan, head of China energy research at the Oxford Institute for Energy Studies noted in a recent report that Chinese stakeholders appeared to correctly guess how much apply they need to maintain economic operations. 

China may have learned this lesson the hard way. In late 2021, the country saw an energy crisis as a result of a global coal shortage. Chinese power companies lost money not only because of high prices, but also because the Chinese government capped how much they were able to charge consumers for electricity. As a result, power plants shut down, and China suffered severe power outages.

China has since poured investments into electrification, as well as oil and coal reserves, but Meidan questioned how and when Chinese stakeholders will make decisions on managing the country’s strategic oil reserves.

“How low could imports (and refinery runs) go before China must tap into its stocks more meaningfully or resume crude buying even at higher costs?” Meidan said in the report. “What does this mean for product supplies and to what extent can coal-to-chemicals offset the loss of oil-based chemicals? And what is driving these decisions?”

There are also other variables counterbalancing rising energy prices, Societe Generale noted, including an apparent willingness for the U.S. to continue to export oil, as well as evidence the Strait of Hormuz is actually allowing greater passage to shipping vessels than initially estimated. But regarding these exceptions, the analysts warned, energy costs will not remain depressed should the conflict continue.

“The market will require higher prices to restore balance,” Haigh wrote. “Several structural pressures are pointing in the same direction: strategic reserves will need to be rebuilt, inventories are unlikely to remain comfortable without incremental supply, and new production requires stronger returns to move forward.”

A TON OF THINGS HAPPENED IN THE STOCK MARKET TODAY.

Here's a full recap:

1. Iran announced a full shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz following U.S. strikes on southern Iran, sharply escalating tensions in one of the world’s most critical oil-shipping routes. Iranian state media also said Iran’s military attacked the U.S. Fifth Fleet in Bahrain in response to the strikes. President Trump is now holding a White House Situation Room meeting to discuss potential additional strikes, per Axios. Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, today said: "We will be hitting Iran hard tonight, they have had their time to negotiate and these attacks are not a restart to the war but to set the terms for the deal."

2. Oracle $ORCL reported FY26 capex of $55.7B, above its prior $50B plan, after Q4 capex reached $15.9B, and guided FY27 capex to a massive $90B–$95B, with around $70B expected to be cash capex as it ramps AI data center spending. The company also plans to raise roughly $40B through debt and equity, including its $20B ATM program, to fund the buildout. In Q4’26, Oracle reported revenue of $19.2B, slightly above estimates and up 21% YoY, adjusted EPS of $2.11, beating estimates and up 24% YoY, operating income of $8.6B, ahead of expectations, and cloud revenue of $9.9B, up 47% YoY.

3. Palantir $PLTR CEO Alex Karp made several notable comments on CNBC about Anthropic, AI labs, Maven, and why Palantir is winning with enterprises. On Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, Karp said “most of the things they talk about in public are running on Palantir,” adding that Amodei has gone from being “way behind” to “ahead” and that he genuinely believes what he is saying. Karp also said the public has only seen older versions of Maven, arguing Americans would be “positively surprised” by the advances in U.S. defense technology. More broadly, he criticized AI labs for operating with what he called a “hyper-religion of hyper-optimism,” saying they believe they can solve all problems — including human nature and even the problems they create themselves. His bigger point was that the models alone do not create value; the real value comes from implementation, deployment, and turning AI into actual workflows, which is why Palantir is winning with enterprises.

4. U.S. inflation came in line with expectations in May, but remained elevated. Headline CPI rose 4.2% YoY, matching estimates, while core CPI increased 2.9% YoY, also in line with expectations. It marks the hottest inflation reading since 2023, but because both headline and core CPI matched what the market was looking for, the report was more about confirming inflation remains sticky than delivering a major surprise.

5. Citadel Securities is making a simple point in its new “Tokenomics” note: AI may be revolutionary, but it still has to obey economics. As token bills rise, companies are being forced to think about compute costs, power, cooling, memory bandwidth, and inference budgets. Citadel says the market is already shifting toward cheaper “good enough” models, creating a split where only the biggest players can afford frontier AI at scale. 

6. OpenAI is reportedly considering major price cuts for its AI services as it looks to win more enterprise customers from Anthropic, per WSJ. The company is weighing significant reductions to token pricing, a move that could pressure profit margins across the AI industry given the massive compute costs required to run advanced models. The push comes as Anthropic’s Claude Code has gained traction with software engineers and helped the company rapidly grow revenue, forcing OpenAI to compete more aggressively on price. A broader AI pricing war could benefit customers by lowering costs, but it may also make the economics tougher for both OpenAI and Anthropic as enterprises look for cheaper ways to use AI at scale.

7. The top 10 most active options today by contracts traded were $TSLA with 3.6M contracts, $NVDA with 3.2M contracts, $AAPL with 1.7M contracts, $AMZN with 821K contracts, $SMCI with 696K contracts, $MSFT with 604K contracts, $META with 579K contracts, $ORCL with 569K contracts, $MU with 529K contracts, and $HOOD with 506K contracts. Tesla led the market with more than 3.6M options contracts traded, followed by Nvidia at 3.2M and Apple at 1.7M.

8. Trading in leveraged and inverse ETFs just hit a record. Total notional volume across U.S.-listed leveraged and inverse ETFs surged to $90B on Tuesday, the highest ever and more than triple the level from a year ago. That volume represented roughly 50% of all AUM in the entire leveraged/inverse ETF universe. The 3x short semiconductor ETF $SOXS alone traded more than 1.3B shares, marking the third-largest single-session volume for any U.S.-listed ETF in the last 20 years. The only bigger ETF volume days came from $QLD and $SSO, both during the 2008 Financial Crisis.

9. OpenAI is reportedly in talks to lease a proposed 10-gigawatt data center campus on federal land in Ohio, per The Information. The project would be built by SoftBank’s SB Energy on Department of Energy land in southern Ohio, with OpenAI controlling the equipment under a 20-year lease. Payments would begin once the site becomes operational, with the first phase expected in 2028. Nvidia $NVDA is expected to supply the hardware and provide a financial guarantee tied to both OpenAI’s lease obligations and SB Energy’s financing, further deepening Nvidia’s role in the AI infrastructure buildout.

10. PIMCO is warning that a new credit-loss cycle is underway, with defaults likely to rise sharply among lower-quality borrowers, including leveraged companies and parts of private credit. The firm says markets may be too complacent about the risks, especially as AI-driven disruption could pressure heavily indebted businesses that are already vulnerable. Against that backdrop, PIMCO favors intermediate-term government bonds, citing recession risk, persistent uncertainty, and the potential for future central-bank rate cuts.

11. SpaceX’s IPO has reportedly drawn demand for more than 4x the shares available, underscoring massive investor appetite for the listing. The company is aiming to raise roughly $75B at a $1.8T valuation, which would make it the largest IPO ever and surpass Saudi Aramco’s record. Shares are expected to begin trading on June 12 under the ticker $SPCX.

12. JPMorgan said U.S. equity futures saw $21B of net selling last week, with most of the pressure coming Friday in S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 contracts. However, that selling was more than offset by $26.8B of ETF inflows, showing investors were still putting money to work despite the volatility. Fixed income ETFs also saw strong demand, while commodities and some emerging market funds faced redemptions. Overall, JPMorgan said investors rotated across sectors but remained broadly long equities even as market swings picked up.

WALL STREET IS THE GREATEST SHOW ON EARTH.

6月11日全球财经晨报：原油库存大降，通胀与利率压制风险偏好，AI基础设施订单继续升温

【美股】

隔夜美股三大指数集体收跌，科技成长板块承压更明显。

道指跌1.87%。

标普500跌1.62%。

纳指跌1.98%。

核心变量仍是通胀韧性与美债利率上行。美国10年期国债拍卖得标利率升至4.538%，高于前值4.47%，成长股估值短线继续受到利率约束。

【AI存储链】

AI基础设施仍是科技线中相对清晰的主线。

甲骨文2026财年Q4营收192亿美元，云收入99亿美元，并预计2027财年营收目标为900亿美元。公司同时披露Q4签署大额AI基础设施合同，说明云计算、服务器、光通信、供电制冷等资本开支链条仍在扩散。

OpenAI与甲骨文合作，使前沿模型和Codex更易于甲骨文云基础设施客户访问。AI应用正在从模型竞争，扩展到云资源、企业部署、开发者工具和软件生态。

A股映射方向：光模块、服务器、数据中心、电力配套、企业软件。

关注线索：新易盛、中际旭创、工业富联、中科曙光、浪潮信息、科华数据、金山办公、用友网络、恒生电子。

【日韩股市】

亚太风险偏好偏弱。

韩国KOSPI早盘一度跌约4%，反映隔夜美股回调与外部不确定性对区域市场情绪的冲击。

日本大型制造业信心指数转负，外资连续卖出日股，日股短线也面临情绪降温与资金流出压力。

【贵金属】

贵金属隔夜大幅回撤。

现货黄金一度跌破4100美元/盎司，早盘进一步跌破4050美元/盎司。

沪金主力夜盘收跌3.37%。

沪银主力收跌1.08%。

黄金当前进入“利率压制与避险支撑并存”的高波动阶段。美债利率上行压制无息资产估值，但避险情绪扰动尚未完全消退，后续重点看实际利率、美元指数和ETF持仓变化。

A股映射方向：黄金、白银、有色资源。

关注线索：山东黄金、紫金矿业、赤峰黄金、银泰黄金、盛达资源。

【原油】

原油是今天最核心的商品线索。

EIA数据显示，截至6月5日当周，美国商业原油库存减少722.7万桶，降幅大于市场预期。

战略石油储备减少792.7万桶，库存降至2023年8月以来低位。

美国国内原油产量升至1379.9万桶/日。

炼厂开工率升至95.3%。

WTI原油盘中上破93美元/桶，库存收缩、高开工、运输风险溢价共同放大油价弹性。后续重点看库存去化是否延续、实际到港量、运费指数和炼厂开工能否继续相互验证。

A股映射方向：油气上游、油服、油运、化工。

关注线索：中国石油、中国石化、广汇能源、中国海油、中海油服、中远海能、招商轮船、中远海控。

【宏观】

美国5月CPI同比升至4.2%，创三年高位，核心CPI亦保持偏强。

市场仍倾向于6月维持利率不变，但能源价格、关税净收入、财政赤字变化，使通胀路径更难线性下行。

加拿大央行连续第五次维持基准利率在2.25%，并强调政策将根据数据灵活调整。全球央行进入“数据依赖+能源约束”的复杂窗口。

对权益市场而言，“不急于宽松”的预期仍是估值压缩的重要变量。

【国内线索】

国内5月居民消费价格同比上涨1.2%。

中汽协披露，5月汽车产销分别为155.4万辆和149.6万辆，同比分别增长22.4%和14.4%。

促进铁路与旅游融合发展的政策发布，消费修复继续呈现结构性特征。

两市融资余额减少80.91亿元，说明风险偏好阶段性降温，资金可能更关注低估值、高股息、防御资产。

A股映射方向：汽车、出行、文旅、高股息、服务消费。

关注线索：长安汽车、比亚迪、中国中免、中国国航、中国移动、长江电力。

【科技产业】

工信部发布“人工智能+信息通信”实施意见，提出到2028年城域算力1毫秒时延圈覆盖率不低于75%。AI与信息通信融合进入基础设施落地阶段。

台积电5月营收达4169.8亿元新台币，前5月销售额同比增长30%，半导体制造、先进封装和高端装备仍是硬科技长期方向。

新易盛筹划发行H股并在香港联交所上市，光模块龙头国际融资路径受关注。

A股映射方向：算力低时延、光模块、服务器、运营商、半导体设备、先进制造。

关注线索：中际旭创、新易盛、工业富联、中芯国际、北方华创、华虹公司、沪电股份。

【今日主线】

1、原油：库存大降叠加外部不确定性升温，短线弹性增强，但追高需看库存和运费验证。

2、贵金属：黄金急跌后波动率升高，趋势修复需要美债利率回落配合。

3、AI算力：订单和政策仍强，但高估值资产仍受利率压制，优先看业绩兑现和订单可验证环节。

4、A股防御与内需：高股息、服务消费、汽车链条在外部波动与利率上行阶段相对更容易获得资金关注。

5、商品链分化：焦煤复产、棕榈油库存上升、甲醇港口库存下降，黑色、油化工、农产品节奏不一致。

【重点日程】

待定：欧佩克公布月度原油市场报告。

15:00：商务部召开6月第2次例行新闻发布会。

20:15：欧洲央行公布利率决议。

20:30：美国5月PPI、美国至6月6日当周初请失业金人数。

22:30：美国至6月5日当周EIA天然气库存。

【一句话总结】

今天市场主线是：原油库存大降推升能源弹性，通胀和美债利率压制风险偏好，黄金进入高波动阶段，AI算力仍有产业订单支撑，但交易上更需要关注估值、利率和业绩验证。

【风险提示】

当前市场同时受到能源价格、通胀韧性、美债利率与外部不确定性影响，商品与成长股波动率均可能维持高位。

贵金属急跌不代表风险完全释放，原油急涨后也需防供应预期反复。

请严控杠杆、分散持仓，避免基于单条消息追涨杀跌。

仅供研究学习，不构成投资建议。

#财经晨报 #原油 #黄金 #美股 #AI算力 #A股 #通胀

BREAKING: Oil Prices Drop as Trump Signals Potential Iran Deal

Crude oil prices fell near $86 a barrel on Friday, hitting their lowest level in nearly two months after President Donald Trump said a peace deal with Iran could be reached as early as this weekend.

The statement came after he postponed a planned military strike and warned that the US could target Iran's oil infrastructure.

Iran's semi-official Fars news agency reported that Tehran is likely to accept the deal, although no final text has been agreed upon by both sides.

Trump said the agreement would reopen shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and include a commitment from Iran not to develop nuclear weapons.

Traders remained cautious, however, as even a breakthrough would face significant obstacles before oil flows could fully return to normal, including clearing mines from Hormuz, restarting dormant production fields, and repairing energy facilities damaged by drone and missile attacks.

The USA is the Top Oil Exporter. The USA just became the world’s top oil exporter at approximately 10.5 million barrels per day

The total Strait of Hormuz traffic accounted for ~20-25% of the total global seaborne oil trade before the recent US-Iran war started. With the recent conflict and blockade in place, this traffic decreased by ~80-95%

So, I ask grok if this is true:

Partially true, but overstated and conspiratorial in its conclusions. The core observations about large U.S./global crude inventory draws and a disconnect with futures prices during the 2026 Iran conflict/Hormuz disruptions are directionally accurate, as is Chris Martenson highlighting suspicious large sell orders. However, the "largest in history" claim is exaggerated for U.S. data (though global draws are extreme), prices have responded significantly (not "barely flinches"), and "market captured/manipulated" is an interpretive leap common in Martenson's analysis rather than proven fact.
https://t.co/gAGSrgegNU
Inventory Draws
U.S.      level: Record single-week commercial + SPR draws occurred, e.g., 17.8      million barrels in one May 2026 week (highest on record per EIA/Reuters).      Multiple weeks of 7–9+ million barrel draws in spring/early summer 2026      amid high exports and refinery runs. This is among the steepest paces in      decades, driven by the Iran war/Hormuz issues.
https://t.co/gAGSrgegNU
Global/OECD:      Draws of 100–250+ million barrels over months (e.g., March–April 2026),      heading toward multi-decade lows. OECD inventories projected to hit lows      not seen since 2003. IEA/EIA describe this as record or unprecedented pace      due to ~8–11+ mb/d Middle East supply disruptions.
https://t.co/IL8raWbttu
This part checks out as unusually large.
Oil Prices and "Barely Flinches"
Not accurate. Prices spiked sharply post-U.S./Israeli strikes on Iran (late Feb 2026) and Hormuz blockade:
Brent      hit $120+ at peaks; WTI over $100–110 in spots.
As of      mid-June 2026: Brent ~$88–94, WTI ~$86–90 (down recently on ceasefire      hopes/rolls). Still elevated vs. pre-conflict ~$70s, but volatile and      lower than worst-case fears.
https://t.co/08bNSbTw1k
Futures have responded to news (spikes on escalation, drops on de-escalation talk). Physical markets (dated Brent) showed much tighter premiums. The "disconnect" exists due to futures paper trading, SPR/emergency releases (~400 mb IEA), floating storage drawdowns, and U.S. export surges—but prices did not stay flat at $92 during full blockade/bombardment.
https://t.co/JmCh4UFlsm
Chris Martenson's Comments
Martenson (Peak Prosperity) has repeatedly warned on this in 2026 videos/interviews. He describes watching massive one-minute sell orders (3–6 million barrels equivalent) slamming prices down, calling it manipulation to suppress prices amid physical tightness. He ties it to broader suspicions of government/futures market intervention for political reasons.
https://t.co/WVuGtXz5tv
Large block trades happen in oil futures (liquid market), and "slamming the tape" is a known pattern in volatile commodities. CFTC has probed suspicious shorts before announcements. Whether this equals systemic "capture" vs. hedging, algos, or legitimate selling is debated—Martenson leans heavily toward intentional suppression.
https://t.co/2fRPYselIP
Broader Context
Hormuz/Conflict:      Yes, Iran blockaded in response to strikes; U.S. countered with its own      blockade. Massive supply shock (~20% of global seaborne oil affected),      offset partly by releases, rerouting, and U.S. shale exports.
https://t.co/YfdMomEPTA
Why      the disconnect? Futures are forward-looking and influenced by expected      reopening timelines, demand destruction, SPR buffers, and speculation—not      pure physical spot. Governments do intervene (e.g., SPR releases      historically). Manipulation claims recur in commodities but require      evidence beyond large trades.
Martenson's      track record emphasizes peak oil/systemic risks; he's often bearish on      smooth market function during crises.
Bottom line: Record draws + physical stress amid Hormuz chaos = real. Price suppression via large sells observed by Martenson = plausible pattern he highlights. Full "captured market ignoring reality" narrative = interpretive and not conclusively proven. Oil markets are complex, with paper vs. physical dynamics, geopolitics, and policy tools at play. Prices have moved but volatility and buffers prevented a total spike.

Daily Commodities Wrap-Up: June 11, 2026

Grains
The grain complex endured a highly volatile Thursday session as traders scrambled to digest the June USDA WASDE report. The agency largely left domestic row-crop balance sheets alone, but global supply additions kept a firm lid on any bullish aspirations. 

Corn futures gave back their mid-week gains, sliding back below the $4.20 line after the USDA boosted South American corn production projections adding 3 million metric tons to Brazil and 2 million metric tons to Argentina. 

Soybeans fared worse, tracking lower as old-crop export estimates were shaved by 20 million bushels, offsetting a matching increase to domestic crush. 

Wheat managed to find rare positive territory after the USDA cut U.S. winter wheat production by 18 million bushels, primarily targeting the hard red winter wheat crop.

Corn (July '26) - Open: $4.20 1/4, Close: $4.16 1/2, -$0.03 3/4, Trend: Bearish, Support: $4.12, Resistance: $4.22
Soybeans (July '26) - Open: $11.16 1/2, Close: $11.11 3/4, -$0.04 3/4, Trend: Bearish, Support: $11.05, Resistance: $11.20
Wheat (Chicago July '26) - Open: $5.89 1/2, Close: $5.92 1/4, +$0.02 3/4, Trend: Neutral, Support: $5.80, Resistance: $6.02

Market Note: The lack of an aggressive domestic cut from the USDA confirms that structural bears remain in total control. Speculative desks are already looking past this report, shifting focus entirely to the high-stakes June 30 Acreage and Quarterly Stocks data.

Livestock
The cattle complex took a breather on Thursday, consolidating just under recent contract highs after a blistering mid-week run. Traders spent the session processing very light direct cash trade in the country, where premium numbers are being defended aggressively. 

Steers in early regional checks averaged a solid $254.15, while heifers remained entirely unoffered. The underlying biosafety landscape remains highly supportive as the industry monitors the rigid six-case screwworm boundary in Texas and the resulting live-animal restrictions out of Mexico. 

Choice boxed beef cutouts eased slightly but held firm in the grand scheme, wrapping up yesterday afternoon at $393.29.

Live Cattle (June '26) - Open: $250.35, Close: $249.50, -$0.85, Trend: Bullish, Support: $246.50, Resistance: $251.50
Feeder Cattle (Aug '26) - Open: $356.20, Close: $354.85, -$1.35, Trend: Strongly Bullish, Support: $351.00, Resistance: $358.50
Lean Hogs (June '26) - Open: $93.10, Close: $93.35, +$0.25, Trend: Bearish, Support: $92.00, Resistance: $94.50

Supply Note: Despite the minor board correction, physical feedlot leverage remains ironclad in the northern plains. Packers are fighting a multi-front battle: they face an incredibly thin uncommitted inventory up north and severe transport compliance delays coming out of southern distribution points.

Oil & Energies
WTI crude oil prices chopped through a tense, macro-driven Thursday, finishing near $90.44 a barrel. Energy desks balanced a massive fundamental tailwind against intense macro anxiety, as persistent headline warnings from Washington regarding potential military operations against Iran kept the broader commodity space on a knife-edge. On the supportive side, the market continues to react to yesterday's massive 7.2-million-barrel collapse in commercial crude inventories, which marks the eighth consecutive weekly drop in domestic stockpiles.

Crude Oil (WTI) - June '26, Open: $90.48, Close: $90.44, -$0.04, Trend: Volatile/Neutral, Support: $88.50, Resistance: $92.00
Natural Gas - June '26, Close: $3.09 1/4, -$0.09 1/4, Trend: Bearish Tilt, Support: $2.98, Resistance: $3.18

Metals
Precious metals moved sideways in a cautious holding pattern, weighed down by a firmer U.S. dollar index holding near 100.3 and a hotter-than-expected Producer Price Index (PPI) print that signals persistent inflationary pressure.

Gold (June '26) - Close: $4,345.50, -$6.70, Trend: Neutral, Support: $4,280, Resistance: $4,400
Silver (July '26) - Close: $68.85, -$0.25, Trend: Bearish, Support: $66.50, Resistance: $70.50

Inside Look
The USDA did exactly what we expected for June: they punted the major domestic adjustments down the road and let the South American production increases do the talking. Remind your followers to keep their eyes locked on performance tracking from Monday's open; this mini-flush post-report shows the funds are perfectly happy defending their heavy short positions whenever a minor rally tries to spark.

The Wheat Lifeline: The 18-million-bushel cut to the domestic winter wheat crop mostly in the hard red winter varieties is the only thing keeping the grain floor from completely falling out today. It provides a localized cushion, but until corn finds a reason to stop leaking, wheat's upside remains tethered.

The South American Shadow: Adding a combined 5 million metric tons of corn production to Brazil and Argentina reminds us that the global market isn't worried about U.S. supply. With domestic emergence pacing beautifully, local elevators are going to have to get creative to move old crop before harvest space becomes an issue.

Cash Cattle Standstill: Packers are trying to use today's minor board pullback to break the feedlots' asking prices, but the country isn't bowing. With cash steers already printing $254.15 on light volume, northern yards know packers have to bite the bullet before the week is out to keep those chain speeds moving.

Notes
Heading into the final trading session of the week, all eyes on the grain side will track whether July corn tests the critical $4.12 contract low or if short-covering returns for a weekend exit. For the livestock pits, the Friday showdown will center on whether the remaining major packers cave to the higher country asking prices or attempt to carry light inventories into next week.

A rainy week in Alberta and one power outage just became a global oil story🛢️

That's not weather news. 

That's what a market with zero slack looks like  when buffers are gone, trivial disruptions move world prices. 

Heavy rain slowed oil sands mining. 

A power outage at Cenovus's Foster Creek/Christina Lake knocked 10% of its production offline force majeure declared.

Result: Western Canadian crude inventories at their lowest since 2020, down 8M barrels since the war began.

🇨🇦Canada is being pulled in 2 directions at once.
Asia wants Canadian crude as "safe" supply  Trans Mountain is at full capacity for the first time ever. 

Meanwhile US Midwest refiners, with no access to seaborne barrels, depend on the same Canadian oil.

1 supplier, 2 customers.

💡Now stack the buffers being drained simultaneously:

US crude stocks: -79M bbl since February, Cushing near operational lows. 
China living off its tanks. 
Atlantic diesel cushion spent. 
Now Western Canada too.
Every shock absorber is being used at once.

I broke down what this means for market positioning .

Do not miss my article.

Oil markets witnessed another roller-coaster session as Brent crude slipped below $91/bbl after President Trump announced the cancellation of planned strikes on Iran and signalled progress toward a broader regional peace framework.
The sharp reversal highlights how geopolitical headlines, rather than fundamentals, are currently driving price action. While hopes of de-escalation eased immediate supply concerns, risks remain elevated as the Strait of Hormuz situation remains unresolved and regional tensions persist.
Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories fell by 7.2 million barrels last week, well above expectations, while Strategic Petroleum Reserve levels continue to decline. The combination of tighter inventories and persistent geopolitical uncertainty suggests oil market volatility is far from over.

Everyone still focused on the noise when the fundamentals are what is driving price. #Crude still at same spot as it was a week ago. Everyone needs to thank China for stepping up this summer, but even with China being the adult in the room, US inventories are approaching key levels in July-August. 

That’s what you should circle, not tweets, war or “ceasefires”. Until then, I don’t think Oil is going to go much higher or lower than current levels. If you hear someone saying manipulation just translate that into “I’m an idiot who cannot make money in the oil market and go max leverage on news/tweets instead of fundamentals”. 

Patience, quit going max leverage on tweets. The time to max it out is when commercial crude inventories dip below 400M or China starts importing Oil again at higher levels. We are 4-6 weeks away, Brent just needs to hold $90 until then.

text:
June 10, 2026 Economic Calendar Recap: CPI In Line with Slight Core Cooling Today’s key release was the US May CPI report, which came in largely as expected: Headline CPI rose 0.5% MoM (matching consensus)
Core CPI (ex-food & energy) came in softer at +0.2% MoM (better than +0.3% expected)
Year-over-year, headline CPI accelerated to 4.2% from 3.8%, while core held at 2.9%

The data shows persistent inflation pressures (especially in shelter and energy), even as monthly core readings moderate slightly. This keeps the Federal Reserve in a data-dependent stance with limited urgency for near-term rate cuts. Other Notable Data: Canada BoC held its policy rate at 2.25% as widely expected, with a steady press conference.
EIA Crude Oil Inventories showed a larger-than-expected draw of -7.23M barrels, supporting oil prices.
Russia and UK inflation figures also released with limited market impact.

Market Implications:
The combination of sticky headline CPI and strong oil prices adds to a cautious backdrop. Tech and growth stocks faced rotation pressure today, while energy names benefited. Markets remain focused on sector rotation, geopolitical risks, and upcoming earnings. Bottom Line: No major surprises in CPI, but the re-acceleration in headline inflation prevents a dovish shift. Traders should stay nimble with key support/resistance levels in major indices.

What’s your view on today’s CPI — enough to change Fed expectations? Drop your thoughts below!

Explosions have been heard off the southern coast of Iran near Bandar Abbas and Sirik as the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) Navy actively enforced its declared chokepoint shutdown with live military action. 
A military source confirmed that the blasts were directly linked to Iranian forces intercepting and confronting an oil tanker attempting to enter the Strait of Hormuz without prior coordination. While Iranian state media acknowledges blocking the tanker, the military source suggested the loud explosions heard near the coastal cities of Sirik and Bandar Abbas were tied to the operation.

Russia's Crude Oil Production Falls to One-Year Low +++

Russia's crude oil production fell to its lowest level in a year last month, according to the Kyiv Independent news site, citing OPEC data. Russian producers manufactured 9.009 million barrels of crude oil per day in May, 690,000 barrels less than the production target agreed upon in the OPEC+ agreement. The reason for this is likely the Ukrainian attacks on Russian energy infrastructure: According to the financial news agency Bloomberg, Ukrainian forces carried out at least 31 attacks on Russian refineries, oil export terminals, and pipeline infrastructure in May, the highest number since the start of Russia's major offensive against its neighbor.

🔴 Russia's crude output hits one-year low as Ukraine strikes oil facilities

Russia pumped 9.009 million barrels of crude per day in May, down from April and 690,000 bpd below its OPEC+ target, according to OPEC data released June 11. The decline coincided with Ukrainian intensification of energy infrastructure strikes.
• Ukraine carried out at least 31 strikes against Russian refineries, oil terminals, and pipelines in May—the highest monthly total since Russia's full-scale invasion began.
• Russian crude-processing rates fell to their lowest level in two decades so far in June, per EA Analytics estimates.
